request.question: What year was the man whose car failed to arrive to the 1950 Indianapolis 500 first the Italian champion?

title: Giuseppe Farina
Jeff Wood (racing driver)
Olson (constructor)
Snowberger
Wetteroth
Charles Van Acker
Langley (constructor)
Andy Michner
Josele Garza
1950 Indianapolis 500
sentences: Dottore Emilio Giuseppe "Nino" Farina (30 October 1906 – 30 June 1966), was an Italian racing driver and was the first official Formula One World Champion, gaining the title in 1950.
He was also the Italian Champion in 1937, 1938 and 1939.
During his thirty-year racing career he suffered a series of accidents.
Jeff Wood (born January 20, 1957) is an American former race car driver born in Wichita, Kansas.
He was Formula Atlantic Rookie of the Year in 1977.
He drove in the CanAm series for Carl Haas in 1981 and finished 5th in the series behind Danny Sullivan.
In 1982, driving for Bob Garnetson Racing, finished 3rd place in the 24 Hours of Daytona.
He then made his CART debut in 1983 at the Caesars Palace Grand Prix driving for Dan Gurney.
He then drove in the Formula Atlantic West Series where he won the 1985 championship.
He returned to CART in 1987 and made 4 starts for Dick Simon with his best finish being 10th.
He was away from the series in 1988, then returned in 1989 to make 5 starts for Gohr Racing, where he finished 12th at the Michigan 500.
In 1990 he drove for a new team, Todd Walther Racing, where he made his first attempt to qualify for the Indianapolis 500, but crashed in practice.
He made 10 other starts, with a 10th-place finish at Phoenix, finishing 22nd in points.
In 1991 he made 8 starts for Dale Coyne Racing, Arciero Racing, and Euromotorsports with the best finish being 13th.
7 starts in '92 for Arciero netted Wood a 12th-place finish and a single point.
In 1993 Wood attempted a nearly full season for Andrea Moda Formula/Euromotorsports but failed to qualify for 6 races and only made 8 starts and again failed to score points.
He made four more unsuccessful starts in 1994 for Euromotorsports in what would be his final races in the series.
He was named to an entry for the 1996 Indianapolis 500, but the car failed to appear.
His best finish in his 49 CART races was an 8th place that came in his second series start back in 1983 at Laguna Seca Raceway.
Olson was a racing car constructor.
Olson cars competed in one round of the FIA World Championship - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Snowberger was a racing car constructor.
Snowberger cars competed in one FIA World Championship race - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Wetteroth was a racing car constructor.
Wetteroth cars competed in one FIA World Championship race - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Charles Van Acker (14 March 1912 Brussels – 31 May 1998 South Bend, Indiana) was a Belgian-American racecar driver.
He first attempted to qualify for the Indianapolis 500 in 1946 but was too slow.
In 1947 he made the race and finished in 29th after a crash on lap 24.
He also competed in seven more races of the national trail that season and finished 4th in points.
In 1948 he 11th in the Indy 500 and 10th in the National Championship.
1949 saw him crash 10 laps into the Indy 500 and struggle to qualify much of the rest of the season.
He attempted the 1950 Indianapolis 500 but failed to qualify in what would be his last Championship Car appearance.
Langley was an American racing car constructor.
Langley cars competed in one FIA World Championship race - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Andy Michner (born October 27, 1968, Ann Arbor, Michigan), is a former driver in the Indy Racing League IndyCar Series, NASCAR Craftsman Truck Series and NASCAR Busch Series.
He is the current record holder of the world's fastest Sprint Car race at a United States Auto Club event in Phoenix, Arizona at 136.034 mph 1996.
Michner finished twice a runner-up to NASCAR'S Tony Stewart in United States Auto Club competition and has 19 USAC Wins.
He passed his Indy 500 Rookie Orientation Program but elected to not qualify for the race 1996 Indianapolis 500.
In 1996 & 1997, Michner ran a partial season in the NASCAR Craftsman Truck Series as Chevrolet's Development Driver.
He then returned to the Indy Car series in 1998 with Konica/Syan Racing and captured his career best finish of eighth place in his first race, the 1998 Indianapolis 500.
Michner then signed with Factory Riley&Scott Reebok Indycar Team where Michner led in the closing laps of the 1998 Texas Longhorn 500 but failed to finish due to an engine failure.
At Michigan International Speedway in August 1998, it was announced, Michner signed a 3-year contract to drive the Bayer Aleve, Coca-Cola Chevrolet in NASCAR's Busch Series for BACE Motorsports.
Michner suffered ultimately career ending injuries in October 1998 while testing at Homestead-Miami Speedway in a NASCAR Busch Series test.
He attempted to qualify for the 1999 Indianapolis 500 for Byrd Racing but failed to make the field due to rain.
He was named to a Logan Racing entry to two races in 2000 but the car did not appear at either race.
Josele Garza (born March 15, 1962 in Mexico City, Mexico) is a Mexican professional race car driver.
He started seven Indianapolis 500 races.
Garza was the 1981 Indianapolis 500 Rookie of the Year after rolling off sixth and finishing 23rd at the age of 19.
At 19 years, two months, and nine days, Garza is the second youngest driver to compete in the Indianapolis 500 and was not surpassed until 2003 by A.J. Foyt IV.
USAC rules at the time required drivers to be 21 years of age, but Garza's racing license listed him as being 22 years old.
Garza claims he does not know how the error occurred but he did not notify officials of the error.
He finished 10th in the 1984 Indianapolis 500.
He led 13 Indy 500 laps.
The 34th International 500-Mile Sweepstakes was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway on Tuesday, May 30, 1950.
The event was part of the 1950 AAA National Championship Trail.
It was also race 3 of 7 in the 1950 World Championship of Drivers and paid points towards the World Championship.
The event, however, did not attract any European entries for 1950.
Giuseppe Farina originally planned to enter, but his car never arrived.
The Indianapolis 500 would be included on the World Championship calendar through 1960.

candidate.summarize1.predict:

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Agent role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

Pipeline position: after summary1 and before second-hop retrieval. Inputs are the original question and the first-hop intermediate state. Query quality is determined by evidence recovery, not by whether the query itself resembles or contains the final answer.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include every distinct entity token visible in the inputs in the single terse query and place at most one neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the targeted relation or attribute (for example: "EntityA EntityB director").
- If a visible disambiguator or attribute token appears adjacent to an entity in the inputs (for example a multiword service name, an explicit page title, a year, or an attribute marker like a birth/producer/service/series cue), include that disambiguator token together with the associated entity in the query rather than dropping or truncating it.
- If `summary_1` contains an explicit page-title token sequence (for example an instruction-like ExactPageTitle or Fetch page title), prefer that exact page-title token sequence as the query value.
- When the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise plain-text summary string (no extra fields, labels, markup, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification.
- Primary goal: explicitly integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the supported linkage it needs.

Explicit bridging requirement:
- When the passages supply a clear linking fact between the context entity and information in the passages, produce a concise 1–2 sentence integrated summary that: (1) names the context entity using the exact form present in `context`, (2) names the supporting entity or attribute using the exact form present in `passages`, and (3) states the explicit relation (membership, creator attribution, parentage, partnership, award, representation, founding, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Phrase this as one short bridging sentence when possible.
- For relations that naturally involve two distinct facts (for example, parentage plus representation, or a person linked to a show and also to an occupation), it is acceptable to use two short sentences within the same single-string summary: e.g., one sentence for parentage and one for representation. Still observe the 1–2 sentence limit.

Inclusion of passage-supplied disambiguators (only when directly relevant):
- If a passage supplies canonical multi-token names or other disambiguating attributes needed to resolve the question, include them inline using the wording from the passages: append birthdates in parentheses immediately after the associated full canonical name (e.g., Name (YYYY–) ), state group type and formation year using the passage's phrasing (e.g., "a [group type] formed in [year]"), and include a canonical member list exactly as shown (comma-separated) only when the passage presents a definitive membership list. Do not add ancillary attributes that are not necessary to the identification or explicit linking required by the question.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (e.g., "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages, comma-separated if multiple. Do not add explanatory clauses, relations, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (for example: "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Preserve uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 short sentences). Do not expand into biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Use only facts and phrasing present in `context` and `passages`; do not introduce or infer unsupported information.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.
- Do not assert that a passage's member list is complete unless the passage explicitly states it is definitive.

Format enforcement:
- Return a single plain-text string only. Do not emit JSON, dictionaries, labels, bullets, or any structured artifact—only the concise natural-language summary string.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else. Before emitting that string, you may apply only the narrowly permitted normalizations listed below. After applying permitted normalizations, the emitted string must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim (see normalization constraints). Specific allowed single-token answers: for a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no. For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces), subject only to the permitted normalizations. Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if a permitted normalization applies (see below).

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) and the permitted normalizations as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Permitted normalizations and extraction rules (apply only when the token(s) to emit appear verbatim in the prioritized summary and only to produce a strictly smaller, but still verbatim-supported, minimal label):
- Parenthetical/core-name stripping: if the candidate label in the prioritized summary includes common corporate suffixes or parenthetical abbreviations (for example variants like "Inc.", "LLC", or parenthetical acronyms), strip those suffixes/parentheticals and emit the core name only (do not invent or substitute other words).
- Hyphenated composite phrase extraction: if the prioritized summary presents a composite genre or label in the form "<modifier> X-Y" (for example an extra leading modifier followed by a hyphenated core like "classic action-adventure"), emit only the hyphenated core token exactly as shown ("X-Y").
- Trailing generic-descriptor removal: if the candidate minimal answer appears as a proper-name followed by a single trailing generic descriptor token of the form "<ProperName> crisis" (or an equivalent single-word generic classifier), strip the trailing generic token and emit only the proper-name sequence.
- Denomination-occupation reduction: if the question's scope requests a denomination/kind and the prioritized summary contains the form "<denomination> priest" (or equivalent denomination + occupational noun), emit only the denomination token(s) (i.e., the minimal denomination label).

Normalization constraints:
- Do not apply normalizations unless the text to emit appears verbatim in the prioritized summary (summary_2, or summary_1 only if summary_2 is silent for the answer).
- Normalizations may only remove or trim tokens; they must not add, invent, or substitute new content.
- Do not globally strip generic classifier nouns from multi-token geographic names or other proper nouns unless covered by the permitted rules above.

Tie-break and variant-selection rules:
- If a role-descriptor and a proper name co-occur in the prioritized summary in the form "<role> <Name>", prefer emitting the named individual exactly as shown (emit the Name only) unless the prioritized summary presents the role as the requested concise label explicitly without a name.
- For title or label-variant conflicts, prefer an alternate or parenthetical variant only when the prioritized summary explicitly marks that variant as an alias, alternate name, or the original-language title (or otherwise explicitly labels it as a variant) AND that variant matches the question's scope. If no such explicit labeling appears, emit the primary label supported by summary_2.

Strict emission rule (verbatim-emission):
- The final `answer` must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim after applying only the permitted normalizations above. Do not append or prepend occupational nouns, role descriptors, location qualifiers, or explanatory tokens that are not part of that verbatim label.

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: What year was the man whose car failed to arrive to the 1950 Indianapolis 500 first the Italian champion?
hop1_docs: 1950 Indianapolis 500 | The 34th International 500-Mile Sweepstakes was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway on Tuesday, May 30, 1950.  The event was part of the 1950 AAA National Championship Trail.  It was also race 3 of 7 in the 1950 World Championship of Drivers and paid points towards the World Championship.  The event, however, did not attract any European entries for 1950.  Giuseppe Farina originally planned to enter, but his car never arrived.  The Indianapolis 500 would be included on the World Championship calendar through 1960.

2001 Indianapolis 500 | The 85th Indianapolis 500 was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway in Speedway, Indiana on Sunday, May 27, 2001.  Race rookie Hélio Castroneves, a three-year veteran of the CART series, led the final 52 laps and won his first Indy 500.  Penske Racing swept 1st-2nd with Gil de Ferran the runner-up.  Winning car owner Roger Penske scored his 11th victory at the Indianapolis 500, and his first-ever 1-2 finish in the race.  It was a redemption from the team's previous attempt at Indy (1995) in which both of his cars failed to qualify; subsequently followed by a five-year absence (1996-2000) due to the open wheel "split."

Chuck Ciprich | Charles J. Ciprich (Born November 30, 1941) is an American former racing driver from Sayre, Pennsylvania.  A successful modified stock car racer who won numerous championships throughout the northeast, Ciprich tried his hand at Championship Car racing in 1983 and 1984.  He first attempted to qualify for the 1983 Indianapolis 500 in an unsophisticated Chevrolet powered Finley chassis fielded by Pace Racing.  He made his CART Champ Car debut later that year at Pocono Raceway but qualified near the back of the field and was knocked out after 38 laps by a transmission failure.  Ciprich and the Pace team arrived for the first oval race of the 1984 season at Phoenix International Raceway with a more advanced, yet year-old March-Cosworth combination yet failed to make the field.  Their attempt to qualify the car for the 1984 Indianapolis 500 was equally unsuccessful and Ciprich returned to Modifieds.  Ciprich retired some years later and now resides in Florida.

Charles Van Acker | Charles Van Acker (14 March 1912 Brussels – 31 May 1998 South Bend, Indiana) was a Belgian-American racecar driver.  He first attempted to qualify for the Indianapolis 500 in 1946 but was too slow.  In 1947 he made the race and finished in 29th after a crash on lap 24.  He also competed in seven more races of the national trail that season and finished 4th in points.  In 1948 he 11th in the Indy 500 and 10th in the National Championship.  1949 saw him crash 10 laps into the Indy 500 and struggle to qualify much of the rest of the season.  He attempted the 1950 Indianapolis 500 but failed to qualify in what would be his last Championship Car appearance.

Bob Frey | Robert C. Frey (born October 25, 1950) is a former American racing driver from Elyria, Ohio.  He was a notable sprint car driver who won some of the country's most prestigious races when he attempted to try his hand at CART Championship Car racing in 1980.  His first attempt to qualify for a race at the Pocono Raceway ended in a practice crash.  His first race came in September of that year at the Michigan International Speedway and he finished 17th.  The following year Frey notched his best CART finish of 13th at Phoenix International Raceway but then failed to qualify his eight-year-old Eagle-Offy for the 1981 Indianapolis 500.  He returned the week after the Indy 500 and drove in the Milwaukee Mile race, finishing 17th.  Frey returned in 1982 with a much newer 1981 Eagle chassis but failed to qualify for both the Indy 500 and the Milwaukee race, after which he returned to sprint cars.

1978 USAC Championship Car season | The 1978 USAC Championship Car season consisted of 18 races, beginning at Phoenix on March 18 and concluding at the same location on October 28.  The USAC National Champion was Tom Sneva and the Indianapolis 500 winner was Al Unser.  This was the last year before the first USAC/CART "Split".  By winning the Indianapolis 500, Pocono 500, and California 500, Al Unser swept the Indy car "Triple Crown", the only driver in history to do so.

Phil Caliva | Phil Caliva (born January 1, 1945 in Palermo, Sicily) is an Italian-born American racing driver who competed in the CART Championship Car series from 1979 to 1984.  After racing in the North American Formula Super Vee series in 1977 and 1978, he made his CART debut in 1979 driving for Spike Gehlhausen at Ontario Motor Speedway.  Caliva returned the next year and made five starts for Bill Alsup and tried but failed to qualify for the Indianapolis 500.  Caliva attempted a second time to qualify for the "500" for Alsup in 1981 but again failed to make the field.  He made 3 more starts later that year, including his best series finish of 8th place at the Michigan International Speedway in July.  He tried 3 races including the Indy 500 in 1982 but failed to qualify for all of them.  He tried the Indy 500 again in 1983 and missed the field again, but made the race at Riverside International Raceway in what would be his last Champ Car start.  He tried the Long Beach race and the Indy 500 again in 1984 but again missed both races.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Giuseppe Farina — [1]: "Giuseppe Farina originally planned to enter, but his car never arrived."
- 1950 Indianapolis 500 — [1]: "May 30, 1950."

Ambiguities:
- "Italian champion" — unclear whether this means "first Italian World Drivers' Champion" or "champion of an Italian national series".
- Name variant: "Giuseppe Farina" may appear as "Nino Farina" on other pages.

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages do NOT state the year Giuseppe Farina was first the (or an) "Italian champion" — the specific year of his first Italian/championship title is missing.

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch page: "Giuseppe Farina" (Wikipedia biography page; a.k.a. "Nino Farina")
- Fetch page: "List of Formula One World Drivers' Champions"

- Query: "Giuseppe Farina Italian champion year"

hop2_docs: Giuseppe Farina | Dottore Emilio Giuseppe "Nino" Farina (30 October 1906 – 30 June 1966), was an Italian racing driver and was the first official Formula One World Champion, gaining the title in 1950.  He was also the Italian Champion in 1937, 1938 and 1939.  During his thirty-year racing career he suffered a series of accidents.
Giuseppe La Farina | Giuseppe La Farina (20 July 1815 in Messina – 5 September 1863 in Torino) was an influential leader of the Italian Risorgimento.

Formula One drivers from Italy | There have been 98 Formula One drivers from Italy including two World Drivers' Champions.  Giuseppe "Nino" Farina won the first ever World Drivers' Championship and Alberto Ascari was the first double world champion.  All three championships came in the early 1950s and very few Italian drivers have come close since Ascari's 1953 victory.  Four of the most experienced Grand Prix drivers in history come from Italy.

1950 Indianapolis 500 | The 34th International 500-Mile Sweepstakes was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway on Tuesday, May 30, 1950.  The event was part of the 1950 AAA National Championship Trail.  It was also race 3 of 7 in the 1950 World Championship of Drivers and paid points towards the World Championship.  The event, however, did not attract any European entries for 1950.  Giuseppe Farina originally planned to enter, but his car never arrived.  The Indianapolis 500 would be included on the World Championship calendar through 1960.

1950 Formula One season | The 1950 Formula One season was the fourth season of the FIA's Formula One motor racing.  It featured the inaugural FIA World Championship of Drivers which commenced on 13 May and ended on 3 September, as well as a number of non-championship races.  The championship consisted of six Grand Prix races, each held in Europe and open to Formula One cars, plus the Indianapolis 500, which was run to AAA National Championship regulations.  Giuseppe Farina won the championship from Juan Manuel Fangio and Luigi Fagioli.

1951 Pau Grand Prix | The 1951 Pau Grand Prix was a non-championship Formula One motor race held on 26 March 1951 at the Pau circuit, in Pau, Pyrénées-Atlantiques, France.  The Grand Prix was won by Luigi Villoresi, driving the Ferrari 375.  Louis Rosier finished second and Giuseppe Farina third.

2007 FIA Formula One World Championship | The 2007 FIA Formula One World Championship was the 61st season of FIA Formula One motor racing.  It featured the 2007 FIA Formula One World Championship, which began on 18 March and ended on 21 October after seventeen events.  The Drivers' Championship was won by Ferrari driver Kimi Räikkönen by one point at the final race of the season, making Räikkönen the third Finnish driver to take the title.  An appeal by McLaren regarding the legality of some cars in the final race could have altered the championship standings, but on 16 November, the appeal was reportedly rejected by the International Court of Appeal, confirming the championship results.  Räikkönen entered the final race in third position in the drivers' standings, but emerged as champion after the chequered flag, a feat first accomplished by Giuseppe Farina in .  It has since been accomplished again, by Sebastian Vettel, in .
response.summary_2: Giuseppe Farina was the Italian Champion in 1937.
